event: The Blessed Bernardo Tolomeo's Intercession for the End of the Plague in Siena
symbols: kneeling monk in white — the intercessor praying for divine mercy amid disaster
angel or death figure above — divine judgment or the angelic minister of plague
plague victims strewn about — the devastation of pestilence and human mortality
dark city in background — the city under the shadow of death
description: A dark, dramatic painting depicting a plague scene. In the center, a figure in a white monastic habit kneels in prayer amid numerous sick and dying figures. Bodies lie on the ground in various states of distress. Above, a dark winged figure hovers against the sky. In the background, a city with walls and towers is visible under a gloomy sky. The palette is predominantly dark — browns, blacks, and muted flesh tones — with the white habit of the central figure providing the main point of light.
theological_description: Crespi depicts the Blessed Bernardo Tolomeo (1272-1348), founder of the Olivetan Order, who ministered to plague victims in Siena during the Black Death and himself died of the plague. His selfless service embodies Christ's teaching: 'Greater love has no one than this: to lay down one's life for one's friends' (John 15:13). The plague was widely understood in medieval theology through the lens of biblical precedent — the plagues of Egypt (Exodus 7-12), the pestilence sent upon Israel after David's census (2 Samuel 24:15), and the apocalyptic horseman of Revelation: 'They were given power over a fourth of the earth to kill by sword, famine and plague' (Revelation 6:8). Bernardo's intercession mirrors Moses standing between the living and the dead to stop a plague (Numbers 16:48) and Abraham interceding for Sodom (Genesis 18:23-32). His willingness to enter the midst of contagion rather than flee reflects the Good Shepherd who 'lays down his life for the sheep' (John 10:11), in contrast to the hired hand who 'abandons the sheep and runs away' (John 10:12-13). The scene affirms that even in catastrophic suffering, the faithful presence of one who prays can be a channel of divine mercy.

scripture_refs.reason: Laying down one's life — Bernardo died serving plague victims
Standing between the living and the dead to stop a plague — Bernardo's intercessory role
Death and plague riding over the earth — the devastation depicted in the scene
The Good Shepherd laying down his life — Bernardo refusing to abandon the afflicted
